Devotional.Title: Day 1
Devotional.PrayerNotesPrompt: Use this space to write personal reflections, prayer requests, and notes from today.

Devotional.Title: Day 2
Devotional.PrayerNotesPrompt: Use this space to write personal reflections, prayer requests, and notes from today.

Daily Scripture Summary: Jesus commands believers to go, disciple, and teach all nations.

Devotional.Title: June 3 – Going with Purpose
Devotional.Overview:
In Matthew 28:19–20, Jesus gives what is often called the Great Commission—but it is more than a command, it is an invitation into the very mission of God. The word “go” is not passive; it implies movement, intentionality, and obedience. As we begin this journey to Athens, we are not simply traveling to a new place—we are stepping into a calling that has existed since Jesus first spoke these words. This means our trip is not defined by schedules, flights, or even ministry activities, but by our willingness to be sent and used by God wherever we go.
There is also a deeper layer to this passage that shapes how we approach this experience. Jesus does not say, “go and observe,” or “go and fix,” but “go and make disciples.” That means our purpose is relational, not transactional. We are not going to accomplish tasks or check boxes—we are going to engage people, to listen, to love, and to reflect Christ. This challenges us to release expectations we may be carrying—ideas about what this trip should look like, how impactful we think we need to be, or how comfortable we expect to feel. Instead, we are invited to trust that God is already at work in Athens, and we are simply joining Him.
Finally, Jesus ends this command with a promise: “And surely I am with you always.” This is not a small statement—it is the foundation of everything we are about to do. As we step into unfamiliar places, cultures, and situations, we are not going alone. God’s presence goes before us, walks with us, and remains with us in every conversation, every moment of uncertainty, and every opportunity to serve. This means we can move forward not in fear, but in confidence—not in our own ability, but in His presence.
ReflectionQuestions: (While You Travel to Athens)
Pray: “Lord, align my heart with Your mission, not my own expectations.”
What stands out to you about Jesus’ command to “go”? How does it change the way you view this trip?
What expectations (good or bad) are you bringing into this experience that you may need to surrender?
How does knowing that God is already at work in Athens change your mindset going into ministry?
What fears or anxieties are you carrying into this trip? Take time to name them and surrender them to God.
Where do you tend to rely on your own strength instead of trusting God’s presence?
Ask God to prepare your heart for the people you will meet—before you even arrive.
Throughout the day, reflect on this question: “Am I approaching this as a traveler, or as someone being sent?”
Pray for unity within your team—that you would support, encourage, and strengthen one another.
Devotional.GroupPrayer:
Lord, today we recognize that we are not just traveling—we are being sent. You have called us to go, and we step into that calling with humility and trust. We confess that we often come with our own expectations, plans, and fears, but today we choose to surrender those to You. Prepare our hearts for what lies ahead. Help us to be open, teachable, and sensitive to Your Spirit.
As we enter a new place and culture, give us humility to listen before we speak, to learn before we act, and to love without conditions. Remind us that we are not going alone—that Your presence is with us in every moment. Use us in ways we cannot yet see. Let our attitudes, our words, and our actions reflect You clearly.
Unite us as a team. Give us patience with one another, encouragement for one another, and a shared purpose rooted in You. And Lord, as we go, help us to remember that this is Your mission—not ours. We trust You to lead, to provide, and to work through us for Your glory.
Amen.
Devotional.PrayerNotesPrompt: Use this space to write personal reflections, prayer requests, and notes from today.

Daily Scripture Summary: Paul shares truth with the people of Athens.

Devotional.Title: June 4 – Serving the Least of These
Devotional.Overview:
In Acts 17, Paul enters Athens and does something that is easy to overlook but deeply important—he pays attention. Before he speaks, before he teaches, before he engages, he observes. Scripture says he was “greatly distressed” by what he saw in the city. This distress was not rooted in judgment or frustration, but in a deep awareness of people who were searching, lost, and disconnected from the truth. Paul allowed what he saw to move his heart before it shaped his actions. As we step into Athens ourselves, this becomes our starting point—not activity, but awareness.
When we pair this with Matthew 25:40, Jesus takes this awareness even further. He teaches that serving “the least of these” is not just kindness or generosity—it is a direct act of serving Him. This changes everything. The person who is overlooked, the one who is hurting, the one who may be difficult to engage—Jesus identifies Himself with them. That means every interaction we have carries spiritual weight. We are not just meeting needs; we are encountering Christ in the people we serve.
There is also a deeper transformation happening in us through these passages. It is one thing to intellectually understand that all people matter—it is another to truly see them that way. God is reshaping our vision. He is teaching us to slow down, to notice, and to resist the tendency to categorize people or move past them. In a culture—and even in ministry—it is easy to become task-focused or outcome-driven. But Jesus calls us back to people. This day is not about how much we accomplish, but about how deeply we see and how faithfully we love.
ReflectionQuestions: What stands out to you about Paul’s response when he first sees Athens?
How does the phrase “whatever you did for the least of these, you did for me” challenge or change your perspective?
Who are the “least of these” in the context we are stepping into this week?
What is the difference between seeing people and truly noticing them?
How can we guard against becoming task-focused instead of people-focused during this trip?
2.3 Personal Reflection (Take With You Today)
Pray: “Jesus, open my eyes to see people the way You see them.”
Where do you tend to overlook people in your everyday life? Why?
Ask God to help you slow down today—physically, mentally, and spiritually.
Be intentional: Who is one person today you can truly notice, listen to, and engage with?
Devotional.GroupPrayer:
Jesus, today we ask that You would open our eyes. Just as Paul was moved by what he saw, we ask that You would allow our hearts to be stirred—not with judgment, but with compassion. Help us to see beyond the surface, beyond appearances, beyond assumptions, and recognize the real needs of the people around us.
You have told us that whatever we do for the least of these, we do for You. That truth is both humbling and powerful. Forgive us for the times we have overlooked people, rushed past them, or failed to truly see them. Transform our hearts so that we do not just understand this truth, but live it.
As we go through this day, slow us down. Make us attentive. Give us eyes to notice, ears to listen, and hearts ready to respond. Help us to treat every person with dignity, value, and love—knowing that in serving them, we are serving You. Use us in ways that go beyond what we can see or measure. Let our presence reflect Your presence.
Amen.
Devotional.PrayerNotesPrompt: Use this space to write personal reflections, prayer requests, and notes from today.

Daily Scripture Summary: Jesus had compassion on the crowds and called for workers.

Devotional.Title: June 5 – Compassion Like Jesus
Devotional.Overview:
In Matthew 9:35–38, Jesus is actively traveling through towns and villages, teaching, proclaiming the gospel, and healing every kind of disease and sickness. This passage shows that Jesus’ ministry was holistic—He cared about both the spiritual and physical needs of people. His mission was not distant or detached; it was deeply personal and relational. He saw people up close, interacted with them, and met them where they were. This sets a model for us as we engage in ministry in Athens—we are not just there to accomplish tasks, but to truly see and engage people.
Verse 36 reveals the heart of Jesus: 'When He saw the crowds, He had compassion for them, because they were harassed and helpless, like sheep without a shepherd.' The word 'compassion' here implies a deep, gut-level emotional response. Jesus was not indifferent or overwhelmed—He was moved. He recognized the deeper reality of their condition: they were spiritually lost, vulnerable, and in need of guidance. This challenges us to look beyond surface-level issues like homelessness or addiction and recognize the deeper spiritual and emotional needs people carry.
In verses 37–38, Jesus shifts from observation to invitation. He tells His disciples that the harvest is plentiful but the workers are few, and He instructs them to pray for more workers. This is significant—Jesus invites His followers to participate in what He is already doing. As we serve, we are not just helpers; we are part of God’s response to the needs around us. We are both the answer to prayer and those who continue to pray for more people to join in the mission.
ReflectionQuestions: What stands out to you about how Jesus sees and responds to people?
What keeps us from feeling this kind of compassion in our daily lives?
How can we move from simply noticing needs to being moved to action?
Where might we encounter people today who are 'harassed and helpless'?
How can our group reflect the heart of Jesus together today?
3.3 Personal Reflection (Take With You Today)
Ask God to give you His eyes to truly see people today.
Where do you tend to become numb or disengaged when you see need?
Who is one person today you can intentionally slow down for and listen to?
Pray: 'Jesus, help me feel what You feel and respond the way You would respond.'
Devotional.GroupPrayer: Jesus, we thank You for showing us what true compassion looks like. You did not turn away from people in need, but You moved toward them with love, patience, and care. We confess that sometimes our hearts grow tired or distracted, and we fail to see people the way You do. Today, we ask that You would soften our hearts. Help us to feel what You feel and to respond with courage and kindness. Open our eyes to the needs around us—not just the obvious ones, but the deeper spiritual needs as well. Use us as Your hands and feet today. Let our words, actions, and attitudes reflect Your love in a real and tangible way. And Lord, we pray for more workers—raise up more people who are willing to go, serve, and love in Your name. Amen.
Devotional.PrayerNotesPrompt: Use this space to write personal reflections, prayer requests, and notes from today.

Daily Scripture Summary: Jesus healed the man with leprosy and withdrew to pray.

Devotional.Title: June 6 – Loving the Marginalized
Devotional.Overview:
In Luke 5:12–16, we see one of the most striking and countercultural moments in the ministry of Jesus. A man “covered with leprosy” approaches Him—someone who, in that culture, would have been completely isolated, rejected, and avoided. Leprosy was not just a physical illness; it carried social and spiritual consequences. This man would have been considered unclean, forced to live outside community, and likely had little to no human contact. Yet, despite all of this, he comes to Jesus with both desperation and faith, believing that Jesus has the power to restore him.
What Jesus does next is just as important as the healing itself—He reaches out and touches him. Before the miracle, before the words, there is a moment of connection. Jesus does what no one else would do. He crosses a boundary that others were unwilling to cross. This touch is not just physical—it is deeply personal. It restores dignity, value, and humanity to someone who had likely not experienced it in a very long time. Jesus does not hesitate. He does not recoil. He moves toward the very person everyone else avoids.
This passage challenges us in a deeply practical way as we step into ministry. It is one thing to care about people in theory—it is another to move toward those who are uncomfortable, difficult, or unfamiliar. There are modern-day “lepers” in every society—people who are overlooked, avoided, or pushed to the margins. As we encounter individuals through homeless ministry, outreach, and refugee work, this passage calls us to examine our own responses. Do we keep our distance, or do we step closer? Do we see a problem, or do we see a person? Jesus shows us that love is not passive—it moves toward people, even when it costs something.
ReflectionQuestions: What stands out to you about Jesus’ response to the man with leprosy?
Why do you think Jesus chose to touch the man before healing him?
Who are the “untouchable” or marginalized people in the context we are serving in?
What fears, discomforts, or assumptions tend to hold you back from engaging with certain people?
What would it look like for our group to reflect Jesus’ willingness to move toward others today?
4.3 Personal Reflection (Take With You Today)
Pray: “Jesus, give me the courage to move toward people, not away from them.”
Where in your life do you tend to keep your distance from people who are different or uncomfortable to engage with?
Ask God to reveal any hidden fears, judgments, or assumptions you may be carrying.
Be intentional today: Who is one person you can step toward instead of avoiding?
Devotional.GroupPrayer:
Lord, we thank You for showing us what true love looks like. You did not avoid the broken, the hurting, or the outcast—you moved toward them. You saw people not as problems, but as individuals worthy of dignity, care, and compassion. We confess that sometimes we hesitate. We allow fear, discomfort, or uncertainty to keep us at a distance.
Today, we ask that You would give us courage. Help us to step beyond our comfort zones and move toward people with the same love You have shown us. Remove any fear or judgment from our hearts, and replace it with compassion and humility. Teach us to see people the way You see them—not defined by their circumstances, but by their value.
As we go through this day, give us opportunities to connect, to listen, and to love in real and tangible ways. Let our presence bring dignity, our words bring encouragement, and our actions reflect Your heart. Use us to remind others that they are seen, known, and loved.
Amen.
Devotional.PrayerNotesPrompt: Use this space to write personal reflections, prayer requests, and notes from today.

Daily Scripture Summary: Jesus washed the disciples’ feet and modeled servant leadership.

Devotional.Title: June 7 – Serving with Humility
Devotional.Overview:
In John 13:1–17, we see one of the most powerful and unexpected moments in the life of Jesus. On the night before His crucifixion, knowing exactly who He is and what is about to happen, Jesus chooses to take the role of a servant. He gets up from the table, wraps a towel around His waist, and begins washing the feet of His disciples. In that culture, foot washing was not just a low task—it was the job reserved for the lowest servant. Yet Jesus, fully aware that He is their Lord and Teacher, intentionally steps into that position.
This moment is not just about the act itself—it is about what it reveals. True greatness in the Kingdom of God looks completely different than what the world expects. It is not about status, recognition, or authority—it is about humility, service, and putting others before yourself. Jesus doesn’t just teach humility; He demonstrates it in a way that would have been shocking to everyone in the room. He redefines leadership by showing that the one who leads is the one who serves.
As we step into ministry, this passage becomes incredibly practical. There will be moments on this trip where things don’t go as planned, where tasks feel small or unnoticed, or where serving others may feel inconvenient or uncomfortable. This is where humility is tested. Jesus did not choose to serve only when it was easy or visible—He served in a way that cost Him something. This passage invites us to lay down pride, expectations, and the desire for recognition, and instead embrace a posture of quiet, consistent service. It reminds us that how we serve matters just as much as what we do.
ReflectionQuestions: What stands out to you about Jesus choosing to wash His disciples’ feet?
Why do you think this act would have been so shocking or uncomfortable in that culture?
Where do you personally struggle with humility—in visible ways or subtle ones?
How do we sometimes seek recognition, even in ministry or serving others?
What would it look like for our group to truly reflect servant leadership today?
5.3 Personal Reflection (Take With You Today)
Pray: “Jesus, give me a humble heart that is willing to serve in any way.”
Where in your life do you tend to seek recognition, credit, or control?
Ask God to reveal any pride, entitlement, or expectation that may be present in your heart.
Be intentional today: What is one way you can serve someone quietly, without drawing attention to yourself?
Devotional.GroupPrayer:
Lord, we thank You for the example You have given us. You are our Lord and Teacher, yet You chose to serve in the most humble way. You lowered Yourself, not because You had to, but because You loved deeply and completely. We confess that humility does not come naturally to us. We often seek recognition, comfort, or control, even when we are trying to serve.
Today, we ask that You would shape our hearts. Remove pride from us and replace it with a willingness to serve in any way You call us to. Help us to value people over position, service over status, and obedience over recognition. Teach us to serve not just when it is easy, but when it is inconvenient, unnoticed, or difficult.
As we go through this day, give us eyes to see opportunities to serve, even in the smallest ways. Let our actions reflect Your heart. Unite us as a team in humility, patience, and love. And may everything we do point back to You, not to ourselves.
Amen.
Devotional.PrayerNotesPrompt: Use this space to write personal reflections, prayer requests, and notes from today.

Daily Scripture Summary: Jesus said His Father is always at work, and so is He.

Devotional.Title: June 8 – God is Already at Work
Devotional.Overview:
In John 5:17, Jesus makes a simple but profound statement: “My Father is always at His work.” This truth reshapes how we understand both God and our role in ministry. God is not distant, inactive, or waiting for us to initiate something—He is already moving. He is already present in the lives of the people we will encounter. He is already working in ways we cannot see. This means that when we step into Athens, we are not bringing God with us—He is already there.
This challenges one of the most common pressures we can feel in mission work—the pressure to perform, to produce results, or to make something happen. But Jesus models a different way. He says, “My Father is always at His work… and I too am working.” In other words, Jesus is not acting independently—He is joining in what the Father is already doing. This is the invitation for us as well. Our role is not to control outcomes or force impact, but to remain aware, available, and obedient.
There is a deeper transformation that comes with embracing this truth. When we begin to trust that God is already at work, it changes how we approach every moment. We become less anxious about what we should do and more attentive to what God is doing. We begin to listen more closely, observe more carefully, and respond more faithfully. Ministry becomes less about striving and more about partnering. This allows us to enter conversations, relationships, and situations with a sense of peace and expectancy—knowing that God is ahead of us, beside us, and working through us.
ReflectionQuestions: What stands out to you about Jesus’ statement that God is always at work?
How does this truth challenge the way you typically think about serving or ministry?
Where have you already seen glimpses of God at work during this trip?
What is the difference between trying to create impact and joining what God is already doing?
How can we, as a group, stay attentive to God’s leading instead of rushing ahead?
6.3 Personal Reflection (Take With You Today)
Pray: “God, open my eyes to see where You are already working today.”
Where do you tend to feel pressure to perform, fix, or control outcomes?
Ask God to help you slow down and become more aware of His presence in each moment.
Be attentive today: Where do you sense God inviting you to step in, listen, or respond?
Devotional.GroupPrayer:
Lord, we thank You that You are always at work. Even when we cannot see it, You are moving in the lives of people, in this city, and within our own hearts. We confess that we often try to take control—we try to plan, to fix, and to measure our impact. But today, we choose to trust You.
Teach us to slow down and become aware of what You are doing. Give us eyes to see, ears to listen, and hearts that are ready to respond. Help us not to rush ahead of You or rely on our own understanding, but to follow Your lead with humility and obedience.
As we go through this day, guide our conversations, our actions, and our decisions. Show us where You are working and give us the courage to step into those moments. Let us be faithful, not focused on results, but focused on obedience. And remind us that You are the one who brings transformation—we are simply Your vessels.
Amen.
Devotional.PrayerNotesPrompt: Use this space to write personal reflections, prayer requests, and notes from today.

Daily Scripture Summary: The Gospel is the power of God for salvation.

Devotional.Title: June 9 – Boldness in Sharing
Devotional.Overview:
In Romans 1:16, Paul makes a bold and deeply personal declaration: “I am not ashamed of the gospel.” This is not just a theological statement—it is a reflection of conviction. Paul had experienced the transforming power of the Gospel in his own life, and because of that, he was willing to speak it, live it, and carry it into any environment, no matter the cost. His boldness did not come from confidence in himself, but from confidence in the message he carried.
Paul goes on to explain why he is not ashamed: “because it is the power of God that brings salvation.” This is where our perspective must shift. The power is not in our words, our ability to explain things perfectly, or our confidence in conversation. The power is in the Gospel itself. God is the one who transforms hearts. God is the one who saves. This means that when we feel hesitant, uncertain, or unqualified, we can remember that the outcome does not depend on us—it depends on Him.
There is also a deeper transformation that happens when we begin to live this out. Fear of rejection, fear of saying the wrong thing, or fear of being uncomfortable can often hold us back. But this passage invites us to move beyond those fears and trust that obedience matters more than perfection. As we step into conversations throughout this trip, boldness may not always look like standing in front of a crowd—it may look like a quiet moment of sharing, a simple expression of faith, or the willingness to speak when prompted. God does not ask us to be flawless—He asks us to be faithful.
ReflectionQuestions: What stands out to you about Paul’s statement, “I am not ashamed of the gospel”?
What are some common fears or barriers that keep people from sharing their faith?
How does understanding that the Gospel itself carries power change your perspective?
What does boldness look like in the context of this trip—not just in big moments, but in everyday interactions?
How can we encourage and support each other as we step into opportunities to share?
7.3 Personal Reflection (Take With You Today)
Pray: “Lord, give me courage to speak when You prompt me.”
What fears or insecurities tend to hold you back from sharing your faith?
Ask God to remind you that the power is not in you, but in Him.
Be attentive today: Is there a moment where you feel prompted to share, encourage, or speak truth?
Devotional.GroupPrayer:
Lord, we thank You for the truth of the Gospel and the power it carries. It is not just words—it is the message of life, hope, and transformation. We confess that at times we feel hesitant, afraid, or unsure of ourselves. We worry about what to say, how we will be received, or whether we are capable.
Today, we ask that You would give us boldness—not boldness rooted in ourselves, but boldness rooted in You. Remind us that You are the one at work, and that the power belongs to You. Help us to be faithful in the moments You give us, whether they are big or small.
Give us sensitivity to Your Spirit. Show us when to speak, what to say, and how to say it with love and humility. Let our words be honest, our hearts be sincere, and our actions reflect You. And when fear rises, remind us that obedience matters more than perfection.
Use us today to share hope, truth, and encouragement with those we encounter. Let us not shrink back, but step forward in faith, trusting that You are working in ways we cannot always see.
Amen.
Devotional.PrayerNotesPrompt: Use this space to write personal reflections, prayer requests, and notes from today.

Daily Scripture Summary: Serving others restores dignity and reflects God's compassion.

Devotional.Title: June 10 – Serving the Homeless
Devotional.Overview:
In Isaiah 58, God speaks directly to His people about what true faith actually looks like. The context is important—people were going through the motions of religious activity, believing that outward acts of devotion were enough. But God challenges that mindset and redirects their attention to something deeper. True faith, He says, is not just about rituals or appearances—it is about how we respond to real human need. It is about stepping into brokenness, lifting burdens, and caring for those who are struggling.
This passage brings faith out of the abstract and into the tangible. It calls God’s people to meet practical needs—feeding the hungry, providing shelter, and caring for those who are vulnerable. But this is not just about meeting physical needs; it is about restoring dignity. When someone is overlooked, ignored, or reduced to their circumstances, something deeper is lost. God’s heart is not just that needs would be met, but that people would be seen, valued, and treated with compassion and respect.
As we step into homeless ministry, this passage becomes incredibly real. It challenges us to examine our assumptions and attitudes. It is easy to unintentionally view people through the lens of their situation rather than their identity. But God calls us to something different. He calls us to slow down, to listen, and to engage with people as individuals—not projects, not problems, but people created in His image. This day is not just about what we give—it is about how we give it, and whether our actions reflect the dignity and love that God has for every person we encounter.
ReflectionQuestions: What stands out to you about how God defines true faith in this passage?
What assumptions or perceptions do we sometimes carry about people experiencing homelessness?
What is the difference between helping someone and restoring their dignity?
Why is listening such an important part of serving well in this context?
How can we ensure that we are seeing people, not just situations, throughout this day?
8.3 Personal Reflection (Take With You Today)
Pray: “Lord, give me a heart of compassion that reflects Yours.”
What assumptions or judgments might you need to surrender before engaging today?
Ask God to help you see each person as an individual with a story, not just a situation.
Be intentional: How can you communicate dignity through your words, tone, and body language?
Devotional.GroupPrayer:
Lord, we thank You that Your heart is not distant from those who are hurting—you are near, attentive, and full of compassion. You see every person, every struggle, and every story. Today, we ask that You would shape our hearts to reflect Yours.
Forgive us for the times we have made assumptions, rushed past people, or failed to truly see them. Remove any judgment or indifference from us, and replace it with humility and compassion. Help us to approach each person with dignity, recognizing their value and worth.
As we serve today, guide our interactions. Help us to listen well, to speak with kindness, and to act with genuine care. Let our presence communicate respect, and let our actions reflect Your love. Use us not just to meet needs, but to restore dignity and bring hope.
Amen.
Devotional.PrayerNotesPrompt: Use this space to write personal reflections, prayer requests, and notes from today.

Daily Scripture Summary: Jesus responds to brokenness with grace and truth.

Devotional.Title: June 11 – Ministry in Brokenness
Devotional.Overview:
In this passage, Jesus is placed in a tense and public situation where a woman is brought before Him in a moment of deep shame and exposure. The religious leaders are not concerned with her restoration—they are using her as a tool to trap Jesus. She is reduced to her actions, defined by her worst moment, and placed in the center of a crowd for judgment. But instead of responding with immediate words or accusations, Jesus slows everything down. He creates space in a moment where others are rushing to condemn.
What follows is one of the most powerful displays of both wisdom and compassion. Jesus redirects the attention from the woman to the crowd, challenging them to examine their own hearts. One by one, they leave. In doing this, Jesus dismantles the environment of shame and accusation that surrounded her. Before He addresses her situation, He restores her dignity. She is no longer surrounded by voices of condemnation—she is standing in the presence of someone who sees her fully and still chooses compassion.
Yet Jesus does not stop at compassion alone. After the crowd is gone, He speaks directly to her. He does not condemn her, but He also does not ignore the reality of her life. He offers both grace and truth—freedom from condemnation and an invitation to change. This balance is essential for us to understand as we step into ministry. We are not called to judge or to ignore brokenness—we are called to reflect Christ. That means approaching people with dignity, compassion, and humility, while also pointing toward hope and transformation. In environments where people carry deep pain, trauma, or shame, this kind of presence can make all the difference.
ReflectionQuestions: What stands out to you about how Jesus responds in this situation?
Why do you think Jesus pauses and writes on the ground instead of responding immediately?
How does Jesus change the atmosphere of the moment before addressing the woman?
What does it look like to protect someone’s dignity in real-life situations like the ones we may encounter?
Why is it important to hold both grace and truth together, rather than leaning too far toward one?
9.3 Personal Reflection (Take With You Today)
Pray: “Jesus, help me reflect Your heart—full of both grace and truth.”
Where do you tend to lean more—toward judgment or avoidance—and why?
Ask God to give you wisdom in how you speak, listen, and respond today.
Be aware: How can your posture, tone, and presence communicate dignity and respect?
Devotional.GroupPrayer:
Jesus, we thank You for the way You respond to brokenness—with both grace and truth. You do not rush to condemn, and You do not turn away. You see people fully, and You meet them with compassion, dignity, and purpose.
We confess that we often don’t know how to respond in difficult situations. Sometimes we react too quickly, or we hold back out of fear. At times we lean toward judgment, and at other times we avoid stepping in at all. But today, we ask that You would guide us.
Give us wisdom in every interaction. Help us to slow down, to listen well, and to respond with care. Teach us how to reflect Your heart—protecting dignity while pointing toward hope. Remove fear from us, and replace it with confidence in You.
As we go through this day, let our presence bring peace, not pressure. Let our words bring encouragement, not harm. And let our actions reflect Your love in a way that is real, respectful, and life-giving.
Amen.
Devotional.PrayerNotesPrompt: Use this space to write personal reflections, prayer requests, and notes from today.

Daily Scripture Summary: God’s power is made perfect in weakness.

Devotional.Title: June 12 – When We Become the Least
Devotional.Overview:
In Matthew 25:40, Jesus makes a statement that is both powerful and deeply personal—what we do for others, especially those who are overlooked or in need, is something we are doing directly to Him. This truth elevates every interaction. It means that kindness, compassion, and service are not just good actions—they are acts of worship. But this passage also carries a weight that goes beyond encouragement. It invites us to examine not only what we do, but what we fail to do.
Earlier in this trip, we reflected on seeing Jesus in “the least of these.” But here, we take a step deeper. This passage also reveals something about our own hearts when we choose not to see, not to act, or not to engage. When we overlook people, step past them, or ignore opportunities to love, it is not just a missed moment—it reflects a disconnect from the compassion of God. In those moments, we are not simply failing to serve—we are choosing distance over connection, comfort over obedience.
There is a deeper transformation God is inviting us into here. This is not about guilt or shame—it is about awareness and change. God is shaping how we see people and how we respond to them. He is inviting us to become people who do not overlook, who do not rush past, and who do not remain indifferent. Instead, we are called to be attentive, responsive, and willing. As we move through this day, this passage challenges us to ask: Where have I been blind? Where have I chosen comfort over compassion? And how can I respond differently moving forward?
ReflectionQuestions: How does this verse challenge the way you think about everyday interactions?
Can you think of a time when you may have overlooked someone in need? What kept you from engaging?
What does it reveal about our hearts when we choose not to act?
Why is it often easier to avoid than to step in?
How can we, as a group, become more aware and responsive to the needs around us?
10.3 Personal Reflection (Take With You Today)
Pray: “Lord, make me aware of what I often overlook.”
Where in your life do you tend to move too quickly to notice the needs of others?
Ask God to reveal any areas where you have chosen comfort over compassion.
Be intentional today: When you notice a need, pause before moving on—what might it look like to respond?
Devotional.GroupPrayer:
Lord, we thank You for the truth of Your Word, even when it challenges us. You have shown us that how we treat others matters deeply, and that in serving them, we are serving You. But we confess that we do not always live this way. There are times when we overlook people, when we choose comfort over compassion, and when we fail to respond to the needs around us.
Today, we ask that You would search our hearts. Reveal anything in us that is disconnected from Your compassion. Remove indifference, distraction, and selfishness, and replace them with awareness, humility, and love.
Help us to slow down, to notice, and to respond. Give us courage to act when You prompt us, and sensitivity to recognize those moments. Transform the way we see people, so that we reflect Your heart more clearly in everything we do.
Let this not just be a day of reflection, but a day of change. Shape us into people who do not overlook, but who step in, love well, and live differently because of You.
Amen.
Devotional.PrayerNotesPrompt: Use this space to write personal reflections, prayer requests, and notes from today.

Daily Scripture Summary: Paul finished the race and kept the faith.

Devotional.Title: June 13 – Finishing Well
Devotional.Overview:
In 2 Timothy 4:7, Paul is near the end of his life, reflecting on the journey he has walked with God. His words are not about achievements, recognition, or visible success—they are about faithfulness. He describes his life as a fight, a race, and a commitment to keep the faith. These images remind us that following God is not always easy or predictable. It requires endurance, perseverance, and a willingness to stay committed even when things are difficult or unclear.
As we come toward the end of this trip, this passage invites us to reflect on what it means to finish well. Finishing well is not about having perfect days or doing everything right—it is about remaining faithful to what God has placed in front of us. It is about continuing to show up, to serve, to love, and to respond, even when we are tired, stretched, or unsure. The final days of a mission trip can be some of the most important, because this is where focus can fade or intentionality can slip. This passage calls us to stay present and engaged all the way through.
There is also a deeper layer of reflection here. God has been at work throughout this trip—in moments we expected and moments we did not. He has been shaping our hearts, challenging our perspectives, and inviting us into deeper trust. Finishing well means taking time to recognize what He has done, not rushing past it. It also means considering how we carry this forward. This is not the end of something—it is the continuation of a life of faithfulness. Just as Paul remained committed to the end, we are invited to continue walking in obedience beyond this moment.
ReflectionQuestions: What stands out to you about Paul’s reflection on his life and faith?
What does “finishing well” mean in the context of this trip?
Where have you seen God at work—in yourself, in others, or in specific moments?
What challenges or distractions can make it difficult to stay focused near the end?
How can we, as a group, encourage each other to stay present and intentional through the final day?
11.3 Personal Reflection (Take With You Today)
Pray: “Lord, help me remain faithful in every moment today.”
Take time to reflect: How has God been working in your heart during this trip?
What has challenged you? What has changed in you?
Where might you be tempted to disengage, coast, or lose focus?
Devotional.GroupPrayer:
Lord, we thank You for all that You have done throughout this trip. You have been faithful in every moment—guiding us, stretching us, and working in ways we may not even fully understand yet. We are grateful for the people we have met, the experiences we have had, and the ways You have moved in our hearts.
As we come to the end of this time, we ask that You would help us finish well. When we feel tired, give us strength. When we feel distracted, bring us back to focus. Help us to remain present, intentional, and faithful in every moment You place before us.
Remind us that faithfulness matters more than results. Help us to continue serving, loving, and responding with the same heart we had at the beginning—or even deeper. And as we reflect on all that You have done, prepare us for what comes next. Let this not be the end of something, but the continuation of a life lived for You.
Amen.
Devotional.PrayerNotesPrompt: Use this space to write personal reflections, prayer requests, and notes from today.

Daily Scripture Summary: Believers receive power to witness to the world.

Devotional.Title: June 14 – Sent Back Home
Devotional.Overview:
In Acts 1:8, Jesus speaks to His disciples just before He ascends, giving them both a promise and a calling. He tells them they will receive power through the Holy Spirit, and that they will be His witnesses—not just in one place, but everywhere. The progression He outlines begins locally and expands outward. This is important because it reminds us that the mission of God is not limited to a specific location—it is a way of life that continues wherever we go.
As we return home, it can be easy to think of this trip as something separate—something meaningful, but contained to a specific time and place. But this passage challenges that mindset. The same God who has been at work in Athens is at work at home. The same calling to see people, serve with humility, step toward the marginalized, and live with boldness does not change when our environment changes. What has changed is us. God has been shaping our hearts, shifting our perspectives, and inviting us into a deeper way of living.
There is also a deeper responsibility that comes with this transformation. Experiences like this are not meant to stay as memories—they are meant to produce movement. We are not just returning as people who have seen something; we are returning as people who are sent. The question is not whether we will continue the mission, but how. What will be different in the way we see people? How will we respond to needs we encounter at home? Will we carry the same intentionality, compassion, and awareness into our daily lives? This passage calls us to live consistently—to allow what God has done in us to shape how we live moving forward.
ReflectionQuestions: What stands out to you about Jesus’ call to be witnesses in every place, not just one?
How has your perspective changed throughout this trip?
What are some specific ways God has been working in your heart?
What might make it difficult to carry this mindset back into your everyday life?
What is one intentional way you can continue living on mission when you return home?
12.3 Personal Reflection (Take With You Today)
Pray: “Lord, show me how to continue living on mission when I return home.”
How has God changed the way you see people, need, or your role in His work?
What is one specific area of your life that needs to look different because of this experience?
Ask God for boldness to live out what you have learned, even when it feels uncomfortable.
Devotional.GroupPrayer:
Lord, we thank You for everything You have done throughout this trip. You have been faithful in every moment—teaching us, stretching us, and drawing us closer to Your heart. We are grateful for the people we have met, the experiences we have had, and the ways You have transformed us.
As we return home, we ask that You would not let this be the end of something, but the beginning of something new. Help us to carry what You have done into our daily lives. Give us boldness to live differently, to see people differently, and to respond to needs with the same compassion and intentionality we have experienced here.
Remind us that we are still sent. The mission does not stop—it continues wherever we go. Empower us through Your Spirit to be faithful witnesses in our homes, our communities, and beyond. Let our lives reflect You in a way that is consistent, genuine, and impactful.
Use us, guide us, and continue to shape us as we walk forward with You.
Amen.
